briefing.opening: Good morning. Welcome to the climate briefing for April 2, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Action and Expansion of Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Domestically, the 'Second Reforestation Movement' is actively being promoted to address climate change [1, 3], with the head of the Korea Forest Service emphasizing forests as a core resource for climate crisis response [13]. Across the nation, various organizations and local governments, including the Small Enterprise and Market Agency (SEMAS) [10, 19], Seocho-gu [12], and the National Uljin Ocean Science Center [21], are expanding tree-planting events to practice carbon neutrality in celebration of Arbor Day [28, 29, 30, 31, 40]. Wonju City and Chungnam Province are recruiting participants for the automobile carbon neutrality point system, offering incentives of up to 100,000 won [8, 9, 17, 22, 24, 27], and Cheongju City is also promoting related events [36] to encourage citizen participation. While Hampyeong Yeonghwa School and the Office of Education are declaring net-zero practices and spreading carbon-neutral school and organizational cultures [25, 26, 39], Yangpyeong County is conducting environmental education to raise awareness of the climate crisis in observance of 'World Meteorological Day' [5], highlighting the importance of climate administration [14].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Renewable Energy Transition and Policy Discussions
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to accelerate the transition to renewable energy. Gunsan City is investing 4.9 billion won to implement a convergence support project, installing renewable energy facilities such as solar and geothermal power in 250 locations, aiming for energy self-sufficiency and carbon neutrality [23, 45, 55, 59]. Yeongju City is also expanding new and renewable energy support, offering subsidies of up to 70% to participants and recruiting applicants [46, 47]. Notably, the Korea Water Resources Corporation's (K-water) floating solar power projects are gaining attention as renewable energy transition emerges as a national task, with experts emphasizing the urgent need for government support to expand floating solar [56, 57]. Meanwhile, the Minister of Climate has stated that nuclear power is unavoidable in the current situation and must proceed alongside renewable energy [44, 61], indicating ongoing policy considerations for achieving energy security and carbon neutrality goals [11, 16, 42].

sections.title: 🔴 Intensifying Global Climate Crisis Impacts and Warnings
sections.content: Globally, severe impacts and warnings due to climate change continue. Research indicates that heatwaves caused by climate change are linked to low birth weight [63], and venomous snakes are expected to migrate to densely populated coastlines [62, 78], increasing threats to ecosystems and human lives. Furthermore, reports suggest that climate change is altering turbulence patterns, affecting aviation safety [68], and increasing the risk of natural disasters like wildfires and landslides, impacting roads and ranchers [70, 75, 82]. In the agricultural sector, concerns are rising about climate change's impact on food security, including warnings of Arabica cultivation loss and a surge in lamb prices over the past three years [71, 72]. Reports of widening differences in opinion on climate change between Canadians and Americans [90] suggest the difficulty in achieving social consensus for climate crisis response.

sections.title: 🌍 Global Renewable Energy Expansion and Progress Towards Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: The international community is making progress towards carbon neutrality goals through the expansion of renewable energy. Globally, 692 GW of new renewable energy was added, accounting for 85% of all new power generation [43], and with half of the world's power capacity now composed of renewables, achieving the pledge to triple renewable energy is within sight [53]. Germany is accelerating its renewable energy transition through 'Electricity Market 2.0' [50], and India's Adani Group is constructing the world's largest 30 GW renewable energy park in the desert [51]. In the UK, record wind and solar power generation in March 2026 avoided approximately £1 billion worth of gas imports [95], and analysis suggests that plug-in solar panels could significantly save household energy bills [98]. The groundbreaking of Aston University's Carbon Neutral Energy Centre [91] and New Yorkers' transition from gas stoves to induction stoves [93] demonstrate that the shift to clean energy is occurring across all aspects of life.

sections.title: 📰 Importance of Climate Change Policy, Research, and International Cooperation
sections.content: The importance of policy, research, and international cooperation for climate change response is being emphasized. The Korea Climate Change Research Institute (KCCRI) has signed an agreement with Chuncheon National University of Education to foster talented individuals [2], and Gyeongsangbuk-do Province has expressed its ambition to become a core hub for carbon neutrality by attracting the National Institute of Climate Science [18]. Internationally, national reports on climate change, population mobility, and conflict dynamics have been published in countries such as Burundi, South Sudan, and Somalia [74, 81, 84], and a groundbreaking advisory opinion procedure on climate change is underway at the African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights [88], addressing climate justice and human rights as key issues. Furthermore, the significant mention of inter-state conflicts surrounding the next IPCC report [83, 97] suggests the difficulty in achieving international consensus on climate change response. The Ministry of Interior and Safety is strengthening climate crisis adaptation efforts through proactive drought management and financial support for vulnerable areas [6, 7].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the active domestic and international efforts to address the climate crisis, alongside continuous warnings about the intensifying impacts of climate change. We must strengthen global cooperation based on scientific insights and accelerate actions for a more resilient future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
